documents.title: Duke University public AI hub or guidance

ai_strategy.summary: Duke University's public AI posture is visible through governance guidance, teaching resources, research activity, and enterprise/tool support. The evidence points to a pragmatic R1 approach: encourage AI literacy and research while constraining sensitive data and academic-integrity risks.

key_provisions: Use AI in ways consistent with academic integrity and existing university policy.
Protect sensitive, confidential, student, research, or regulated data when using AI tools.
Faculty or academic units retain responsibility for clear course-level expectations where applicable.
Human review and accountability remain necessary for AI-assisted work.
ai_policy.summary: Duke University has public AI guidance or policy-adjacent materials. The cited guidance addresses responsible use, data protection, ethics, and course or work-context expectations.

bodies.name: AI at Duke Steering Committee
bodies.scope: Coordinates or evidences institutional AI governance, guidance, research, teaching, or tool enablement.

governance_structure.summary: Public evidence identifies AI at Duke Steering Committee as the main AI governance or coordination signal for Duke University.

initiatives.title: AI at Duke Steering Committee AI governance and guidance
initiatives.description: Duke University publishes AI guidance or governance resources that frame responsible use, academic integrity, privacy, and institutional oversight. The cited materials make AI a university-level issue rather than only a classroom technology topic.
initiatives.significance: This is a material public signal of how an R1 university is translating AI from discussion into institutional practice.

investment.description: No public dollar amount was identified for this specific initiative.

collaborations.description: No external collaboration was identified in the cited source.

initiatives.policy_implications: The public guidance creates an institutional baseline for course, research, and administrative AI use.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: Duke University responsible AI and access signal
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: Cited public materials connect AI adoption to privacy, bias, academic integrity, access, community benefit, or responsible human use.

regulatory_context.summary: North Carolina public AI activity is relevant context; this profile treats campus policy and institutional evidence as the primary governance layer.

narrative_summary: Duke University presents an AI posture typical of an active R1 institution: public guidance for generative AI, visible teaching resources, research activity, and a growing set of supported tools or infrastructure. The most mature signals are governance and responsible-use guidance, teaching and syllabus support, and research capacity through AI at Duke and Duke AI Health. AI in education and health research programs gives the institution a workforce-development or curriculum signal, while ChatGPT Edu pilot/licensing shows that AI is also being handled as an enterprise technology and data-governance issue. Public sources also show attention to responsible use, access, bias, privacy, or community impact, though some operational details remain incomplete. In particular, current public pages do not consistently disclose named executive ownership, all procurement decisions, full user counts, or complete dollar amounts. This profile therefore treats the institution as operational and growing rather than fully transformative: AI is embedded across policy, pedagogy, research, and tools, but not every part of the campus AI operating model is publicly documented.

gaps_acknowledged: Leadership names, enrollment, and exact enterprise AI user counts were not filled unless directly necessary; public pages were stronger for initiatives than current-role rosters.
Some initiatives cite policy or hub pages because a single dated strategy document was not publicly identified.
No comprehensive AI budget was identified beyond explicitly cited grants or public funding signals.
Duke now has public ChatGPT Edu/AI Suite and GPU-capacity evidence, but enterprise AI active-user counts and contract costs are not public.
No public student-subgroup outcome metrics from Duke enterprise AI deployments surfaced in this sweep.
